data.title: The Biggest Misconception About REITs
data.description:
Recency bias causes many to incorrectly view REITs as a low return asset class. It is true that REITs have had dismal returns in recent years with Vanguard REIT ETF (VNQ) basically flat for 5 years.

However, extrapolating the recent past misses the bigger picture. In this article I want to discuss 3 concepts which suggest forward REIT returns will likely be significantly better.

  1. Longer term view of REIT returns
  2. Multiple compression
  3. High interest rates versus rising interest rates

Let us examine this in a longer-term context. The furthest back we have been able to chart the REIT index is the start of 1990, so the timeframe here is just chosen by availability of data.

REITs have a fairly low correlation with the S&P but generally have had almost identical returns.

Around the 2000 tech bubble, S&P returns got significantly ahead of REITs. The steady cashflow nature of REITs is often viewed as unexciting so when investors are chasing outrageous returns, REITs usually lag behind.

As the tech bubble unwound, REITs caught back up. Then in the 2005-2007 real estate bubble, REITs materially outperformed. With 20/20 hindsight, real estate prices clearly got ahead of themselves, resulting in REITs crashing harder than the broader market when the bubble popped.

In the depths of the Financial Crisis around 2009, cumulative total return parity was once again restored between REITs and the S&P 500. REITs rebounded harder and outperformed steadily through 2020 right up until COVID hit. The pandemic shutdown disproportionately hurt REITs because certain property segments like office and to some extent retail, were basically not allowed to operate. While office is a tiny portion of REITs now, it was one of the largest property sectors back in 2020.

REITs bounced back swiftly again after the COVID crash, partially aided by the fresh interest rate cuts causing the cumulative performance of REITs to once again overtake the S&P in 2021 (We will go into greater detail on interest rates later).

2022 is when the REIT underperformance began.

Nearly all of it can be chalked up to multiple compression in REITs at a time when the S&P 500 multiple expanded.

Multiple Compression

Multiple compression was responsible for nearly all of the weakness in REITs in the past 4-5 years. In late 2021 the median REIT was trading at 18.2X forward FFO and 18.6X trailing FFO.

Today, the median REIT is at 14.2X trailing FFO.

Since the start of 2022, REIT trailing multiples went from 18.6X to 14.2X.

Over the same time period, S&P trailing multiples went from 23.11X to 29.11X.

  • S&P multiples expanded by 26%
  • REIT multiples contracted by 25%

Thus, the substantial outperformance of the S&P in the last 4 years was almost entirely driven by multiple expansion/contraction.

If one assumes dividends were reinvested and all else remained equal, it is quite simple math to calculate what index returns would look like if multiples returned to where they were at end of 2021 levels.

If we look at the same long term total return chart for REITs and the S&P 500, but adjust their end valuation to the same multiple at which the 2 groups were trading at the end of 2021, REITs would have actually outperformed.

Thus, most, if not all, of the S&P outperformance in recent years was a result of the S&P multiple expanding and REIT multiples contracting.

What happened to multiples – misunderstanding of interest rate mechanisms

Financial math would suggest that all financial assets should price inversely to prevailing interest rates. Higher interest rates imply lower earnings multiples.

The rationale is simple: If one can get X% return from a “risk free” treasury, stocks need to offer a forward return significantly higher than X%. The best proxy for forward returns is earnings multiple. There are of course many other factors that go into the calculation but the inverted multiple serves as the baseline forward return. A 20X multiple is a 5% earnings yield while a 15X multiple is a 6.7% earnings yield.

Therefore, when interest rates rise, earnings multiples are supposed to drop. Well, if we look back to the end of 2021, the 10-year treasury was yielding 1.52%.

The Fed Funds rate was at 0%

Today, we have a 4.29% 10-year Treasury yield and 3.75% upper bound on the Fed Funds rate.

It would make sense for earnings multiples to be lower in today’s environment. REITs got the memo and this is at least partially why FFO multiples dropped from 18.6X to 14.2X.

Somehow S&P multiples went the other way.

That is backward logic from the basic earnings yield math.

It can only make sense if one assumes growth rates dramatically changed. I think this is where the misconception comes in. In my opinion, the market has not differentiated between the impacts of CHANGING interest rates versus the impacts of a higher LEVEL of rates.

Changing interest rates versus the absolute level

Changing interest rates had damaging impacts on REITs. When interest rates moved up 2 things happened:

  1. NAV declined. A building valued at a 7% cap rate is immediately worth less than the same building formerly valued at a 5% cap rate.
  2. Interest expense increased

It was a double whammy for REITs. They lost asset value and earnings power.

S&P assets are less tangible in nature often consisting of IP or operating businesses. In theory, the value of these things would adjust downward in response to higher rates, but many S&P companies aren’t really valued on assets anyway. So the S&P had less of a hit to book value from the interest rate change and even in instances where it happened, the market cared less about it.

Many of the biggest S&P weights had negative debt positions, meaning they carried large amounts of cash. So while REITs lost earnings power from their debt becoming more expensive to carry, some key S&P companies gained earnings power in that their cash hoards which used to earn nothing could be put in money market accounts and earn 4%-5%.

The above are impacts of the change in interest rates. The change was quite rough for REITs.

This is often mistaken with the impacts of the level of interest rates.

We are now stably in a medium-to-high interest rate environment. While rising rates were very bad for REITs, a high level of interest rates is beneficial to REITs.

REITs already paid the price of getting to the higher interest rates and going forward they will get to reap the benefits of a higher level of interest rates. Specifically, a higher level of interest rates conveys 2 key benefits:

  1. Higher cap rates make property acquisitions more accretive.
  2. Higher interest rates reduce construction activity, thereby keeping supply in check

This makes the forward growth rate of REITs much higher than it was in 2021. External acquisition growth is better, and existing properties should have better rent growth due to reduced forward supply.

REIT growth has been anemic the last few years due to the higher interest rates taking a while to trickle into active interest expense. At this point, the new rates are largely in effect, and it will stop being a headwind to growth rates.

I think the market is incorrectly associating the weak growth with high interest rates when the weak growth was a result of rising interest rates.

The headwind of rising interest rates is becoming a tailwind of stable high interest rates, so I suspect the low multiple at which REITs are trading is a bit too low once one considers a faster forward growth rate.

I think the end of 2021 multiple for REITs was a bit too high so I don’t think the multiple will expand all the way to 18X, but 16X would be quite reasonable.

The bottom line

REITs are a high return asset class with the recent period of low returns being an anomaly associated with pain from rapidly changing interest rates combined with substantial multiple compression.

With an already compressed multiple and forward fundamentals bolstered by the now stable higher interest rate environment, REITs are well positioned to return to their historically strong returns.

data.title: NextEra Energy: Iran War Exposes Flaws In Fossil Fuels, Advantages Of Renewables
data.description:
Even before the Iran war, I was critical of the Trump administration's policies to double down on fossil fuels while simultaneously rolling back practically all of the Biden administration's EV, solar, wind, and battery backup initiatives to more effectively compete with China. Nothing has exposed the flaws of the administration's energy strategy as has the war on Iran. It is the biggest disruption and destruction of oil and gas assets in world history, in my view, and has had a very negative impact on the United States and the world. Indeed, despite being the biggest petroleum producer in the world, the United States is not immune from the fallout: gasoline prices have jumped 15% since the war started (see chart below), while the March CPI inflation print surged 0.9% - likely pushing additional interest rate cuts further into the future.

As a result, China is arguably winning the underlying energy war, while the United States seems determined to fritter away the gift of domestic shale oil (more on that later). What is frustrating to many (including myself) is that America seems to know what the solutions are because NextEra Energy (NEE) has become the largest utility company in the United States by specializing in those solutions. And, despite the Trump administration's de-prioritization (if not outright hostility...) toward clean energy, NextEra will continue to be very successful by deploying these low-cost solutions for decades to come. Today, I'll explain why that is the case and will give a preview of NEE's Q1 earnings report, which is due out on April 23rd.

Investment Thesis

An article in Fortune quotes Jacky Tang, emerging markets CIO at Deutsche Bank, as saying that the war on Iran has set off a global race for energy security that makes China stronger (see "Deutsche Bank Says China Is Energy 'Winner' In Age of War"). While acknowledging that China will be tested by its reliance on Iranian oil imports, its status as the largest supplier of clean tech (i.e., EV, solar, wind, and battery backup manufacturing capacity) puts it in a unique position to assist governments around the world to "wean themselves off of Middle East imports." As a result, Tang said:

China is the winner in this war from an economic standpoint, from an energy mix standpoint.

Tang also said, longer term, "everybody knows" that the world "cannot rely on oil."

I agree 100%. In fact, China has rapidly embraced EVs in order to reduce its reliance on foreign oil imports while simultaneously boosting its EV and clean-tech exports as consumers worldwide seek to protect themselves from skyrocketing oil and gasoline prices. As a result, China's March EV exports jumped to a record 349,000 units (+140% yoy). That being the case, from an economic and national security perspective, China is much more strategically positioned for the 21st century as compared to America, which has arguably gotten yet another generation of Americans addicted to ICE-based vehicles that require high-priced oil (gasoline) to run. Meantime, Bloomberg reports that China's clean-tech firms have signaled a windfall from the Gulf energy shock. Just imagine the benefit to the United States if it were both the largest petroleum producer and a significant clean-tech provider, which was exactly the goal of the Biden administration's clean-tech legislation, in my view.

NextEra doesn't manufacture EVs, but the company is America's leader at deploying the solar, wind, and battery backup capacity that is key toward enabling a clean-tech future to wean the U.S. off its addiction to fossil fuels. According to the EIA, the fact is that the United States utility industry has been prioritizing renewables for incremental electric power generation capacity additions for many years now and will do so again this year:

As you can see from the graphic, solar will make up more than half of all new incremental power generation additions this year, while in aggregate the total combination of solar, wind, and battery backup will equate to 93.5% of all new capacity additions (a whopping 79.5 GW). This is not because American utility company CEOs suddenly got religion about the environment. It's because solar, wind, and battery backup are the cheapest and quickest ways to add power generation capacity and can be completed on time and on budget as compared to much more expensive and longer solutions like nuclear and natural gas. In addition, there is a shortage of utility-scale natural gas turbines.

So, with NextEra Energy leading the way, America already knows how to very effectively deploy renewable energy. But America's leaders need to combine this expertise with the critical next step: craft policy incentives to combine renewable electric power generation with EVs and clean-tech manufacturing capacity. That's what China did, and that is exactly what the Biden administration's bipartisan infrastructure legislation did.

Meantime, NextEra offers utility and/or dividend growth investors an attractive 75% regulated, 25% unregulated business mix, which will lead to margin expansion as NextEra flexes operating leverage through renewables while maintaining utility-like stability through its high-quality Florida Power & Light utility (FP&L). As a result, NextEra also offers investors the very attractive combination of a secure dividend and above-average growth potential.

So let's take a closer look at NextEra Energy to see just how it is helping America make the clean-energy transition.

The NextEra Advantage

A slide from NextEra's March presentation shows why NextEra is such an attractive investment going forward:

As you can see from the graphic, NextEra operates one of the largest fleets of nuclear and gas-fired generation. However, it is also a global leader in renewable power generation and battery backup storage solutions while being America's leading transmission infrastructure developer. And, as I pointed out in a previous Seeking Alpha article, NextEra has teamed up with Google (GOOG) to not only restart the Duane Arnold nuclear power plant secured with a 25-year power purchase agreement ("PPA"), but also to utilize Google AI across its entire energy platform in order to improve cost and deployment efficiencies (see "NextEra: The U.S. Has Been Losing 'Electron War' With China, That's Changing").

Critically, NEE says it has secured all the battery backup and solar panel capacity requirements it needs through 2029 while also saying that the vast majority of wind components are sourced from domestic supply chains. So, while other utility companies are waiting on natural gas turbines and the many years it will take to build new nuclear capacity, NextEra is ideally positioned to keep deploying clean tech (solar, wind, and battery backup) at an accelerated rate while continuing to grow Florida Power & Light - the largest electric utility company in America with over 6 million consumers and 37 GW of operating capacity.

Meantime, NEE's NextEra Energy Resources subsidiary currently has 43 GW of total operating generation and storage capacity and a remarkable ~30 GW of renewables and storage backlog (~15 GW were added in 2025 alone), the largest backlog in the company's history and one that continues to grow at a double-digit rate (the 5-year backlog grew at a 17% CAGR):

Indeed, NEE estimates its renewables and storage business alone will triple in size by 2032. Part of that dynamic is that NEE offers speed-to-market and "ready now" solutions that have the company on track to deliver ~6 GW of capacity to some of the leading hyperscalers.

As a result of these dynamics, NEE is estimating an 8%+ CAGR in earnings through 2035, is already targeting the top end of its previous 2026 earnings guidance, and targets a 10% increase in the dividend again this year. It also has targeted dividend growth of 6% for 2027 and 2028.

Earnings

NextEra is scheduled to release Q1 earnings on Thursday, April 23rd. Current consensus estimates, courtesy of Yahoo Finance, are shown below:

As you can see, the average estimate is for $0.98/share, but note the range is huge (high of $1.19, low of $0.65), which likely has to do with the timing of large-scale project contracting and deployment and the quarterly accounting thereof. As a result, I put more credence in the annual estimate of $4.01, which, if realized, would equate to +7.7% yoy.

Note that NEE's previous guidance for full-year 2026 was for $3.92-$4.01, so analysts have already baked in NextEra's recent comments about earnings already trending to the "top-end" of that guidance. Given that we aren't even through the first 4 months of the year, my guess is - given how bullish the electric power generation environment is due to the AI data center catalyst - that NEE will raise FY2026 guidance at least once prior to year-end.

Risks

The Trump administration's "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" poses a direct threat to NEER's long-term backlog via the cuts in solar and wind tax credits. However, note that state and corporate renewable policies help to offset the Trump administration's policies, and, as noted earlier, NEE feels somewhat insulated from the full impact of the "BBB" legislation because it had already started numerous wind and solar projects and locked up supply. However, if the Republicans win again in 2028, this could become a bigger issue.

Unfavorable regulatory decisions are always a risk to utility companies, yet from that standpoint, FP&L operates in one of the friendlier regulatory environments (i.e., the state of Florida).

Severe weather events as well as the potential for a global recession due to the impact of the Iran war also pose downside risks. Rising interest rates and financing due to the war in Iran's impact on inflation could also make it more costly and/or difficult for NextEra to develop NEER's renewable energy backlog.

Upside risks include announcements of additional hyperscaler agreements for large-scale power generation capacity additions for new AI data centers.

Energy Security

A chart of the price of Brent crude over the past 35 years says it all:

As you can see from the extreme volatility on the chart (not to mention its rising price trend over time), this is arguably not what a country wants to rely on for consistent and sustainable economic growth. That is particularly the case when often the events driving these price spikes are often Middle East developments that are out of their control - examples like the 1973 Arab oil embargo, the 1979 Iranian Revolution, or the recent closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

As analyst Jackie Tang was quoted above, over the long term, everybody likely knows that the world cannot rely on oil. China was forced to acknowledge this fact given its huge population moving into the middle class and the rapid rise in its dependence on foreign oil imports to fuel the boom in auto sales. But China was pragmatic and quickly transitioned to adopt EVs and clean energy. Not just for energy security, but for economic security.

The oft-used argument that "we will always need oil and gas" while true, misses the point in my opinion. The simple fact is the faster America reduces its consumption of fossil fuels, the better - not just from an environmental standpoint (although that should be a top consideration), but also from long-term strategic economic and national security perspectives. NextEra Energy has - and will continue - to lead America in renewable energy capacity, but it could do even more with more pragmatic and long-term strategic energy policies.

Unfortunately, the current U.S. administration has adopted what I consider to be a dangerous false sense of security by the fact that the U.S. is now the #1 petroleum producer in the world. Energy experts know that that is due primarily to the shale oil renaissance. Yet I argue that Tier-1 shale inventory is already maturing and, of course, is finite. As Tier-1 shale inventory declines, capital efficiency gains are flattening with diminishing returns from longer laterals and more complex completion recipes. My estimate is that the U.S. has less than 10 years left before the best and most economic shale rock becomes scarce - and that estimate would decrease if the U.S. raises current production even further.

So, where will the U.S. be a decade from now if it continues to rely primarily on ICE-based vehicles? Where will the U.S. economy be if we need to import the vast majority of affordable EVs, solar, wind, and battery backup capacity? Logic says the U.S. will be right back on the OPEC+ leash (like it was in the 1970s) and will be reliant on China for the clean-tech solutions to mitigate the impact of our reliance on oil.

My point here is that I believe the U.S. can ill afford to continue these massive swings in energy policy and to waste valuable time making the energy transition to electrification. Hopefully, future American administrations will come to grips with these simple and sobering facts and get back to pragmatic, smart, and long-term strategic energy policies. And when it does so, NextEra Energy will be leading the charge and will be in an excellent position (just as it is today...) to reap the rewards of being the American leader in renewables. Note this would also be bullish for the entire electric utility sector (XLU).

Summary & Conclusion

I reiterate my BUY rating on NextEra Energy due to its very large backlog, the potential for a rise in earnings guidance this year, and its relatively attractive dividend growth. Investors should consider NextEra as a long-term utility and/or dividend growth holding due to its attractive combination of its unregulated renewables business combined with its high-quality regulated FP&L utility. NEE also offers investors the best of both worlds: a secure dividend (supported by long-term PPA contracts) and above-average growth potential as compared to the utility sector as a whole.

Based on my expectations for FY26 earnings to come in at ~$4.04/share and putting a 25x multiple on those earnings, my 12-month price target for NEE is $101, which equates to a 7.5% return based on Friday's close of $94.08. Add in the current 2.65% yield, and the total return would be 10%+. That's not bad for a utility stock, given the current geopolitical backdrop and yo-yoing market sentiment based on short-term (or perhaps even mid- or long-term...) developments in Iran and the corresponding and somewhat extreme market volatility. The point here is that the utility sector - and particularly NextEra - appears to be a "shelter from the storm." That is especially the case given the bullish AI data center build-out catalyst, which I believe is durable for at least the next 2-3 years.

I'll end by noting that my 12-month price target is ~$5 above the "Moderate Buy" consensus of the 16 analysts included in the TipRanks forecast:

data.title: LNG Shipping Stocks: The Easing Of Tensions Led To A Decline
data.description:
UPI & SPX

The UP World LNG Shipping Index, which tracks 20 listed LNG shipping companies, lost 4.10 points (1.78%), closing at 226.94 points, while the S&P 500 index gained 3.56%. The chart below illustrates the performance of both indices with weekly data.

Broader View

The easing of geopolitical tensions led to a decline in the UPI.

The UPI fell from record highs, and this sharper decline is altering the index’s trajectory. A slowdown is occurring, reflecting 1. the calming of the geopolitical situation, although a resolution is still far off; 2. the end of the winter season; and 3. the decline in spot rates for LNG tankers.

The calming of the situation in the Middle East does not mean the problems have been resolved, as Qatar’s gas infrastructure has not only halted production but has also been damaged by bombing.

According to Reuters, 15 LNG tankers are loaded in the Gulf and ready to set sail as soon as it is safe to do so. Two tankers that attempted to pass through last week turned back after being stopped by Iran’s Revolutionary Guards. According to Alex Froley, Qatar typically dispatches 90–100 tankers per month, while, as reported by LNGPrime, weekly exports from U.S. terminals reached 37 tankers, two fewer than the previous week. These figures indicate growing U.S. exports, with the historical average around 30 tankers per week.

Spot rates continue to decline, with the Atlantic route costing $89,750 per day and the Pacific route $73,000 per day, according to Spark Commodities.

Back to the UPI, which is showing signs of exhaustion. Trading volume was average, though even that has doubled from its usual level after the recent surge. The ratio of rising to falling stocks was 7:13.

Crystal Ball

The second quarter is usually the weakest period of the year. However, geopolitical circumstances and the associated loss of nearly 20 per cent of global LNG production mean that this year will be different.

Europe still has an advantage over Asia; refilling storage facilities can still wait, and greater purchasing power may once again redirect gas from Asia to Europe. Europe, however, needs it now, and the increased—albeit currently falling—prices will be paid by the poorest countries, such as Bangladesh, which is seeking additional spot supplies.

Russia is seeking to capitalise on the opportunity, taking advantage of the U.S. administration‘s current inclination towards its worldview. However, as soon as this reduces U.S. LNG supplies to Asia, the game will be over. Europe is also increasing its imports of Russian LNG, as the U.S.’s sometimes hostile stance is fuelling fears of dependency. Unlike Russia, however, the U.S. remains a democracy.

As we noted last year, it has been worthwhile to monitor the contracts of LNG shipping companies—specifically, which regions they serve. Those operating between the US and Europe, or between Australia and Asia, are more stable.

So what do we expect? The shortfall in LNG supplies will need to be replaced. We view the return to coal as temporary; we expect a gradual increase in spot supplies or, to a lesser extent, long-term contracts with newly commissioned terminals. Increased geographic diversification of suppliers, driven by greater energy security requirements, will lead to longer shipping routes and a greater need for tankers.

The long-term outlook remains positive. The scrapping of steam vessels and the addition of new liquefaction capacity are pushing the sector higher.

Constituents

Although the UPI fell, we’ll start with the rising stocks. There are several interesting ones right off the bat.

First is the nearly 9% rise in Nakilat (QSE: QGTS). The second LNG carrier from the war zone is also rising, albeit later than ADNOC L&S. With average trading volume, Nakilat has moved back into the support zone we’ve seen since the end of last year.

The second point of interest is the 29.3% rise posted by Korea Line Corporation (KRX: 005880). After several weeks of hesitation, KLC has decided to rise and has nearly reached the short-term (unsustainable) peak from the start of the attacks on Iran. Trading volume in this case is above average, but not extreme.

New Fortress Energy (NFE) also posted strong double-digit growth, rising by 23.16%. This is more of a return to high volatility than a reversal of the negative trend. We have discussed the reasons for the current decline in previous reports. Recall that this is a reaction to the restructuring, which diluted existing equity stakes.

Excelerate Energy (EE) is still trading near its support level despite a 5% gain. ADNOC Logistics & Services (ADX: ADNOCLS) rose by 2.69%, reaching older price levels, but it currently remains below the more recent ones. The rally is thus relatively fragile.

MISC (KLSE: 3816), (OTCPK: MIHDF) chart looks very similar to that of the aforementioned Excelerate Energy. MISC is trading in the support zone, with growth above 8.51 ringgit having been suppressed for the fourth week in a row. Last week, the stock rose by one per cent within the support zone.

Declines, though more numerous, were not as pronounced. Chevron (CVX) saw the largest loss, at 5.24%. This marks the second consecutive decline, though it may simply be a correction. The main reasons are geopolitical events and oil price developments.

Most declines hovered around three to four per cent: Mitsui O.S.K. Lines (TSE: 9104, -4.33%), (OTCPK: MSLOY), Capital Clean Energy Carriers (CCEC, -4%), FLEX LNG (FLNG, -4%), Golar LNG (GLNG, -3.64%), COSCO Shipping Energy Transportation (SS: 600026, -2.98%), and Tsakos Energy Navigation (TEN, -2.94%).

With this decline, Mitsui’s growth prospects have diminished, as the chart’s current pattern is indecisive and a sideways trend is at risk. CCEC continues to decline, and the price has reached the support level from 2024–25. During the week, it fell below that level but has since rebounded.

FLEX LNG remains below the $30.50 resistance level and is forming a sideways trend. The future direction is difficult to predict. The decline in spot rates has eroded clear growth drivers, while new long-term contracts have strengthened the company’s future cash flow. The upcoming quarterly commentary will be important, as will developments in spot rates and the overall geopolitical situation. In this regard, FLNG is not alone.

Golar failed to capitalise on the positive sentiment from the week before last, and the share price weakened. This does not clearly signal a change in the upward trend, especially given that trading volume was below average.

COSCO is also moving sideways, although the price continues to creep higher within the sideways range; it has not yet managed to hold above 26 yuan.

Tsakos also paused its upward trend; during the week, both upward and downward momentum were tested. However, neither has yet gained enough traction to determine the direction of the next move.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla: Don't Worry About Inventory (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has seen a major downward revaluation so far in 2026, which was in part driven by a decline in financial expectations amid delivery challenges in the firm’s core market. As a result, investors expected a serious inventory buildup for the first-quarter for Tesla which was aided by the U.S. government eliminating federal tax subsidies for the purchase of an electric vehicle in the third-quarter of 2025.

While Tesla did in fact see a significant buildup in the first-quarter, of about 50,000 units, the EV maker is adjusting its manufacturing processes and Tesla's CEO Elon Musk is guiding for a strategic shift towards autonomous mobility and AI-first robotics. I believe investors have become too bearish on Tesla’s growth prospects and I view the current consolidation as an excellent opportunity to increase exposure to Tesla ahead of the first-quarter earnings report.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Tesla a Sell in my last coverage in February -- The Valuation Makes No Sense -- shortly after the firm’s Q1 ’26 earnings report because the EV platform remained, despite delivery challenges, widely profitable and published respectable gross margins. In the first-quarter, Tesla will likely have experienced a compression of its automotive gross margins -- a key figure for EV manufacturers -- and pressure on its free cash flow given Tesla’s production and delivery imbalance in the first-quarter.

However, I feel that the recent negativity surrounding Tesla has become a little too much and the market is now, ahead of the Q1 '26 report on April 22nd, pricing in excessive levels of pessimism. Given the serious contraction in the valuation multiplier and sentiment change, I am changing my rating to buy, mainly because shares have reached my previous "buy level."

Delivery-production imbalance set to pressure FCF and margins in Q1’26

Despite delivery headwinds in the market, Tesla reclaimed the title of the world's largest BEV seller from BYD (BYD). Tesla delivered 358,023 electric vehicles in the first-quarter, which represented an increase of 6.3% year-over-year, but which nonetheless missed consensus estimates of 370,000 units. On the other hand, BYD, which is China's largest EV manufacturer by volume, had much smaller EV sales of 310,389 units in Q1 '26, down 25.5% year-over-year due to reduced EV buying incentives as well as a new 5% vehicle purchase tax that is applied in China since January 1, 2026.

Due to the large drop in BYD EV deliveries in the first-quarter, Tesla retook the EV crown from its fiercest Chinese competitor in the EV market in Q1 '26. However, as I mentioned previously, BYD has an impressive product line-up in the BEV market and is aggressively focusing on international expansion. In the longer term, BYD is likely going to penetrate the U.S. electric vehicle market as well, especially with highly-affordable and popular EV models such as the BYD Seal, which would appeal to more price-conscious buyers and which could lead to more margin pressure in the EV sector.

Most importantly, Tesla’s production volume fell to 358,023 units in Q1 ’26, highlighting a 50,363 unit inventory surplus which the market expects to weigh on the company's gross margins. Tesla is still leading the EV market in terms of gross margins (see below) and had a gross profit margin in the fourth-quarter of ~20%, nearly 4 PP higher than BYD's margins. While it is likely that Tesla's gross profit margins drop back to around 17-18% in the first-quarter, the company has a number of catalysts that could provide a margin up-lift going forward, including a strategic shift to higher-margin products such as the Model Y (especially the FY 2026 Model Y refresh), robotaxis and Optimus robots, and it recently announced price increases for legacy EV models as well.

Tesla announced in Q1 ’26 that it would wind down operations for its flagship sedan Model S and the Model X... models that represented less than 4% of Tesla's total 2025 production volume. The decision to end production for the low-volume Model S and Model X was followed by an announcement to raise their prices by $15,000 for remaining inventory units which could make a positive margin impact for Tesla in the coming quarters as well. The end of Model S and Model X production has serious implications for Tesla because the EV company is now accelerating its transition to becoming a robotics and AI enterprise… with a core focus on Optimus Humanoid production.

Negative sentiment about inventory buildup now priced into shares of Tesla

Since the middle of December, Tesla has fallen from ~$500 to just about $350 on Monday. The year-to-date return is negative 22% and shares are now trading below my 2025 fair value estimate of $360 per-share. In my opinion, the correction provides investors with yet another engagement opportunity, especially because Tesla is now set to accelerate its transition from an EV company to an AI-focused robotics enterprise.

Tesla is scheduled to report earnings for the first-quarter on April 22, 2026 and the current EPS estimate revision trend is slightly against the EV company due to the inventory buildup: in the last 90 days, Tesla has seen 7 EPS downside revisions for its upcoming Q1 ’26 report which compares against just 4 EPS upside revisions. However, the inventory buildup should be resolved over time as Tesla is adjusting its manufacturing processes to align production more closely with current delivery rates.

Shares of Tesla are currently valued at a forward P/S ratio of 10.9X which is below the 1-year average price-to-revenue ratio of 12.0X. Tesla's shares have been known for serious volatility in the last several years with investors regularly floating between extreme optimism and extreme pessimism, each time offering investors a buy and sell opportunity. The current drop-off is a great chance to buy back into Tesla (or start a new position entirely), in my opinion: assuming that Tesla could revalue to its longer term P/S ratio of 12.0X, shares of the EV and robotics enterprise could have a fair value of $385 per-share (+10% upside revaluation potential).

Right now, Tesla is back in a phase of heightened investor pessimism with regards to its increasing inventory. However, Tesla is proactively addressing this by tapering production of its legacy models and aggressively retooling its manufacturing footprint for a high-margin, AI-driven future. If Tesla can create new investor optimism on April 22, 2026 about its autonomous driving software and the scalability of its Optimus robotics program, then shares could revalue higher.

While Tesla is still a lot more expensive than any other EV manufacturer, the company also has a serious gross margin lead over other companies and retains a robust, debt-free balance sheet that allows it to self-fund the massive CapEx required for its AI-first transformation.

Risks with Tesla

In the short term, Tesla is going to be exposed to delivery headwinds which is the result of eliminated buyer incentives and growing competition in some markets, especially China. The market also seems to already price in a gross margin drop in Q1 '26, so I see Tesla's downside potential from here as limited. What would get me to change my mind about Tesla is if the company were to see a sustained down-trend in gross profit margins (to a BYD-like level of 16% or lower) or if the enterprise were to fail rolling out its robotaxis (expected for 2026).

Final thoughts

The consolidation with respect to Tesla shares provides a unique investment setup for investors, in my opinion, because the market already expects a decline in free cash flow and earnings, given the Q1 ’26 inventory buildup, and likely lower automotive gross margins as well. I believe that Tesla's pivot towards robotics and AI is exactly what Tesla should be doing given growing pressures in the EV market. In this context, any specific robotaxi and Optimus robot guidance in the Q1 '26 report later this month could help investors become more constructive and bullish as well.

Recent price increases for the Models S and X, which are going out of production, are great ways for Tesla to maximize per-unit profitability on its remaining legacy inventory while signaling a broader strategic shift into becoming an AI-native company. If investors see that more revenues are going to shift away from EVs into new revenue lines, such as robotics, Tesla could quickly become an investors' favorite again.

data.title: AIER's Everyday Price Index Rockets 2.5 Percent In March 2026
data.description:
By Peter C. Earle

Household costs are rising faster than mainstream inflation gauges reveal. US military adventurism is hitting Americans hard.

AIER’s proprietary Everyday Price Index (EPI) vaulted 2.5 percent to 307.4 in March 2026, its second-largest monthly increase back to January 2020 (the first was an increase of 2.9 percent in March 2022). Of the 24 price categories that compose the EPI, fourteen rose, two were unchanged, and eight declined. Unsurprisingly, the largest jumps in price occurred in motor fuel, housing fuels and utilities, and food away from home. Prescription drugs, internet services, and food at home declined the most. (The juxtaposition of price changes in the food away from home versus food at home categories likely reflects the gasoline pass-through of food delivery service costs.)

AIER Everyday Price Index vs. US Consumer Price Index (NSA, 1987 = 100)

The US Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) released Consumer Price Index (CPI) data for March 2026 on April 10, 2026. Headline inflation rose 0.9 percent over the past month, meeting survey expectations. Core inflation rose 0.2 percent, also meeting forecasts.

March 2026 US CPI headline and core month-over-month (2016 – present)

Consumer prices in March showed a mixed pattern, with food prices flat overall after February’s 0.4 percent gain, as grocery prices slipped 0.2 percent even while restaurant prices continued to edge higher. Within food at home, most major categories softened, led by a 0.6 percent decline in meats, poultry, fish, and eggs — helped by a 3.4 percent drop in egg prices — while cereals, dairy, and nonalcoholic beverages also moved lower; the main exception was fruits and vegetables, which rose 1.0 percent. The dominant story, however, was energy, which surged 10.9 percent on the month, its sharpest increase since 2005, driven by a record 21.2 percent jump in gasoline prices and a 30.7 percent spike in fuel oil, though natural gas prices bucked the trend with a slight decline.

Core inflation, excluding food and energy, remained comparatively subdued at 0.2 percent, matching February’s pace and suggesting that the broader inflation impulse outside commodities remained largely contained. Housing-related costs continued their steady upward march, with shelter and owners’ equivalent rent each rising 0.3 percent, while rent itself increased 0.2 percent. Several travel- and consumer-sensitive categories also advanced, including airline fares, up 2.7 percent, apparel, up 1.0 percent, and education, up 0.3 percent, indicating persistent service-sector firmness. Offsetting those gains were declines in medical care, especially prescription drugs, along with lower prices for used vehicles and personal care goods. Taken together, the March report points to an inflation profile dominated by a commodity-driven energy shock layered atop still-firm but relatively moderate core and shelter pressures.

On the year-over-year side, the March 2026 headline CPI jumped to 3.3 percent from 2.4 percent the prior month, slightly less than the 3.4 percent predicted. Core prices, which strip out the more volatile food and energy components, advanced 2.6 percent over the same period versus the 2.7 percent survey expectation.

March 2026 US CPI headline and core year-over-year (2016 – present)

From March 2025 through March 2026, inflation remained broad but uneven across major consumer categories. Grocery prices rose 1.9 percent overall, and restaurant prices climbed a stronger 3.8 percent. Within food at home, the largest annual gains came from nonalcoholic beverages, up 4.7 percent, fruits and vegetables, up 4.0 percent, and the “other food at home” category, which increased 2.9 percent, while cereals and bakery products posted a more modest 2.1 percent rise. Offsetting those gains were declines in dairy products, down 1.6 percent, and in meats, poultry, fish, and eggs, which slipped 0.9 percent from a year earlier. Energy remained one of the strongest inflation drivers, advancing 12.5 percent year over year, led by an 18.9 percent jump in gasoline prices, alongside notable increases in electricity and natural gas costs.

Excluding food and energy, core consumer prices rose 2.6 percent over the year, indicating that underlying inflation pressures remained present but far less dramatic than in the commodity-sensitive categories. Shelter costs continued to provide a steady source of upward pressure, increasing 3.0 percent over the past year and reinforcing the persistence of services-related inflation. Other areas showing meaningful annual gains included medical care, household furnishings, recreation, and especially airline fares, which surged 14.9 percent. Taken together, the annual data suggest an inflation environment shaped by still-elevated energy costs, firm service-sector pricing, and selective food price strength, even as some grocery categories offered consumers modest relief.

The March CPI report was, above all, the first clear inflation print to show the economic effects of the Iran war filtering directly into household prices. The most immediate transmission channel was energy, where the jump in oil prices rapidly fed into gasoline and related fuel costs, pushing the headline reading markedly higher and dominating the public perception of the report. This was less a story of generalized demand pressure than of a geopolitical commodity shock suddenly colliding with the consumer economy. In that sense, March’s inflation picture looked more like an external supply disturbance than the kind of broad-based overheating that typically worries central banks most.

Beneath that headline surge, however, the underlying inflation structure remained comparatively calm. Grocery prices were mixed to softer, several major goods categories showed little evidence of renewed pricing pressure, and some discretionary consumer areas even appeared to weaken as households began adjusting to higher costs at the pump. Services inflation, while still firm in key shelter-related components, generally moderated, suggesting that the oil shock had not yet meaningfully spread into the wider service economy. The only obvious early spillover was in travel-sensitive categories such as airfares, where higher jet-fuel costs likely began feeding through almost immediately.

Taken together, the report reads as the opening stage of an energy-led inflation episode rather than a true reacceleration of the broader price trend. The phenomenology is important: consumers are first encountering the shock in the most visible and psychologically powerful places — gas stations, travel, and transportation-linked expenses — while the rest of the basket remains relatively stable. That distinction matters where policy is concerned, as it gives the Federal Reserve room to interpret the move as a temporary oil-driven disruption rather than evidence that inflation is becoming entrenched again. The next few reports will determine whether this remains a contained geopolitical price shock or evolves into something more diffuse across an already-strained household cost landscape.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Iran War: What's Driving Market Sentiment?
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Chief Global Market Strategist and Head of Strategy & Insights

“It depends on the duration of the conflict.” I cannot remember ever hearing a singular phrase as often as I have this one during the ongoing conflict in Iran. I know that I’ve said it more than once. It has become a kind of shorthand explanation for the uncertainty that conflict brings to economies and markets. If we only knew how long this would last, then we could understand the impact on growth, inflation, policy, and markets.

At the outset, many assumed that anything lasting more than a few weeks would be meaningfully negative for risk assets. Now we’re approaching six weeks. And yet, the S&P 500 Index is now nearly flat since the conflict began.1 Credit spreads have tightened.2 Volatility spiked early and then receded.3 Markets did what markets often do - they appear to have absorbed the shock.4

That reality forces a reconsideration of the original assumption.

Narratives versus specifics

Perhaps it wasn’t the specific duration that mattered most. Perhaps it was whether the market would ever succumb to a belief that there was no end in sight. Investors, particularly after last year’s Liberation Day whipsaw, have shown little appetite for pricing in open-ended worst-case scenarios.5 The cost of being overly defensive too early may remain fresh in investors’ memories.

In that context, markets appear to have made a judgment call. They appear to not have priced this as a conflict that spirals endlessly outward. Even talk of a ceasefire, like we got last week - sustainable or not - had been enough to lift risk sentiment. The bar for relief has been surprisingly low.

The terms of any ceasefire remain unclear. Skeptics have been quick to raise issues around tolls collected by Iran at the Strait of Hormuz or the time required to rebuild damaged energy infrastructure. Initial reports suggest that Iran will demand a toll of $1 for every barrel of oil going through the Strait of Hormuz.6 Turning the Strait into a politicized transit route could also raise insurance premiums and financing costs across the energy complex.

These risks are real, but incentives matter. Iran has billions of reasons to normalize energy flows. The region’s Arab producers have strong incentives to rebuild infrastructure quickly and preserve market share. A permanently impaired energy system would hurt everyone involved.

Does that mean we’re heading back to $55 a barrel oil?7 Not necessarily. But does it suggest that prices can come down from recent peaks while remaining elevated relative to pre-conflict levels? That seems likely, in my view. Ultimately, the psychological shift from not knowing whether there’s an end to believing that there’s one may be more important than knowing the exact date. Markets appear to crave narrative closure more than calendar precision, and tend to trade well if/when conditions are simply getting better relative to the deteriorated expectations.

Macro backdrop remains supportive

Beyond the conflict itself, the broader macro backdrop still offers support. Global leading indicators remained resilient.8 Inflation expectations are elevated but contained.9 And after a tepid core inflation report,10 the Federal Reserve may even begin to contemplate rate cuts at some point in the future. This isn’t the pristine Goldilocks environment it felt like at the start of the year, but in my view, it remains one that can sustain risk assets.

Bottom line: Perhaps market implications were never fully dependent on the duration of the conflict. Instead, it may depend on whether investors believed the duration was finite.

data.title: ICOP: The Age Of Copper Is Upon Us
data.description:
The iShares Copper and Metals Mining ETF (ICOP) is a passively managed strategy designed to provide global exposure to companies that participate in copper production and mining. With global demand for increased data centers and electrical infrastructure, copper demand may remain elevated, supporting strong cash generation among the copper mining operators. Until data centers broadly transition to photonics for communications, I believe facilities will continue to rely on copper cabling that can continue to support the market for the coming years. Given the robust outlook and supportive market environment, I am recommending ICOP with a Buy rating.

Investment Thesis

The copper market is positioned to remain durable over the coming years as hundreds of billions of dollars are invested in power transmission and distribution and data center infrastructure, requiring a substantial amount of copper in a trend that I expect to continue over the next decade. In power infrastructure, it is expected that utilities will invest $1.1T through 2030 to modernize and expand grid operations. This is roughly equivalent to the amount invested between 2015-2024, essentially compressing 10 years of investments into half the time. Demand is largely driven by the need for grid stability, reliability, and affordability, as well as for reindustrialization and data center power requirements.

Data center developments are becoming increasingly intricate as grid capacity may no longer support additional data center sites, particularly given that some are scaling beyond 1GW in capacity. With 97GW of data center capacity expected to come online between 2025-2030, power requirements for this segment of the market will double, reaching 200GW in total capacity. Given the sheer size of these newer sites, onsite infrastructure is being deployed to ensure stable power distribution and reliability, requiring everything from battery storage to onsite transformers and switchgear.

A major factor that may deliver sustained copper pricing is the expected supply and demand dispersion in the coming decades. Accordingly, the copper supply is expected to be short 10mm metric tons with respect to demand by 2040. This figure includes not just virgin copper but also recycled copper scrap.

The challenge at hand is that it takes nearly 20 years to commission a greenfield mine when taking into account exploration and discovery, feasibility studies, permitting and approvals, engineering studies, infrastructure construction, service roads and transportation development, all before first ore. Given that a mine cannot simply be spun up may be one of the leading factors for the tight market over the coming years.

This can translate to growth tailwinds across the 46 copper miners held in ICOP, which include Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) at 8.43%, Anglo American (AAUKF) at 8.21%, and Grupo Mexico at 8.04% as the top 3 holdings.

Looking at Freeport-McMoRan, the company is currently undergoing a phased restart and ramp-up at its Grasberg Block Cave underground mine, which is expected to begin production in Q2’26. The company was faced with some challenges near the end of 2025 resulting from a mud rush incident at PT Freeport Indonesia (PTFI). Looking ahead, the firm is undergoing operational enhancements to increase the annualized run rate of copper production from 240MMlbs to 300MMlbs in 2026.

Anglo American is undergoing new mine development initiatives while employing strict cost controls. While management expects production to remain subdued in 2026 with lower production from Collahuasi, they voiced increased production in 2027 and 2028 with increased production at its Chile operations with an additional 125kt of copper. Enhancing its footprint, Anglo American and Teck Resources are currently engaging in a merger, which is expected to close 12-18 months following the announcement in September 2025. The merger is expected to generate $800mm in pre-tax annual synergies, which can deliver stronger profitability going forward.

Overall, I believe the copper industry is positioned to realize continued growth, driven by a sharp increase in demand for copper paired with cost discipline that can deliver stronger cash generation. A potential offsetting factor amongst data centers is the transition towards photonics for communications, though I believe the increased power requirements and the transition to 800-VDC architecture can offset the decline in copper cabling.

About iShares Copper and Metals Mining ETF

ICOP was launched by iShares on June 21, 2023 on the NASDAQ Exchange. The strategy has a management fee of 47bps, a relatively low fee when compared to peer copper mining ETFs like the Global X Copper Miners ETF (COPX) and the Sprott Copper Miners ETF (COPP). ICOP exhibits strong liquidity with $444mm in net assets, with an average of $18mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis.

ICOP is indexed to the STOXX® Global Copper and Metals Mining Index. The index was designed to track companies that derive more than 50% of revenue from copper mining, and it uses a price-weighted approach based on the free-float market capitalization of the constituents.

ICOP has diversified regional exposure, with Canada making up 27.4% of the portfolio, the UK at 20.5%, the US at 18.3%, and Australia at 14.9%.

Risks Related to ICOP

ICOP solely invests in companies that participate in copper mining, potentially exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Given that ICOP is not a diversified portfolio, investors should use the strategy as a component of a diversified investment strategy. ICOP’s global diversification may expose investors to geopolitical risks, trade risks, supply chain risks, and regional pricing risks. Regional macroeconomic factors like interest rate policy and inflation may influence regional producers and may impact the cost of capital for equipment and projects.

Final Thoughts

ICOP can be an appealing investment strategy given the growing market demand for copper for power infrastructure and data centers. With an expected widening of the supply and demand dispersion in the coming decades, copper prices may remain supportive of growth initiatives, resulting in stronger cash generation amongst the miners. Given the market tailwinds, I am recommending ICOP with a Buy rating.

data.title: Sea Limited: Scalable, High Growth Business, But Beware Of Risks
data.description:
The sentiment around Sea Limited (SE) has been very negative in the past months, with the stock price down more than 30% year-to-date. While the sell-off started a while back, the latest earnings release did not make the situation easier for the company, mainly due to the increased loss provisions and elevated costs. I, however, have a much more optimistic view on the firm. Given the exceptional growth figures across all three business segments - e-commerce, digital financial services, and digital entertainment - I believe that investing in Sea's business right now could be an attractive choice for growth-focused investors who want to invest in firms that have a global footprint but are not active in the United States.

Financials - Recap and highlights

Sea Limited reported earnings recently, and they delivered strong results for both the first quarter and for the entire fiscal 2025. Growth was sustained across all three segments they operate in - e-commerce (Shopee), digital financial services (Monee), and digital entertainment (Garena).

On a group level, revenue reached $22.9 billion in FY2025, representing a growth of 36% year-over-year. Gross profit totalled in $10.2 billion, while net income came in at $1.61 billion, both meaningfully above the prior year's results.

Let us break these results down by segment.

Shopee remains the firm's largest revenue-generating segment, responsible for more than 70% of the firm's total revenue. One might think at first that this should be the segment that we pay most attention to. But there is more to it. When it comes to adjusted EBITDA, however, Shopee becomes the smallest segment. Monee and Garena account for much less revenue, but in terms of EBITDA, they together make up more than 75% of the total results.

Let us take a closer look at Shopee first.

Shopee’s GAAP revenue and take-rate both improved substantially. But to put the scale of the business into perspective, we need to look at some numbers. 400 million active buyers and 20 million sellers on the platform, while the firm achieved a GMV of $36.7 billion for Q4, 28.6% higher than in the prior year.

Monee reached 20 million first-time borrowers, with GAAP revenue of $3.8 billion in fiscal 2025, up 60.1% compared to the prior year. While the provision for credit losses increased overall, the percentage of NPL90+ actually decreased by 0.1% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.

Garena reached an average of 100 million daily players. Bookings increased by 37.3%, revenue was up by 26.1% and adjusted EBITDA reached $1.7 billion, representing a growth of 38.1% YoY. Quarterly active users grew by 2.5% in the fourth quarter, compared to the same period in the prior year, while the portion of paying users grew by 1 percentage point.

The Garena segment is also showing improvements in adjusted EBITDA as a percentage of bookings.

From a fundamental perspective, I like that all of the segments are growing rapidly and scaling well. I like that despite the macroeconomic landscape, the company is able to reach a larger and larger audience both in terms of e-commerce, finances, and entertainment. Looking forward to fiscal 2026, management expects growth to continue, and this is going to be one of the main pillars of their operations. For Shopee, GMV is expected to increase by 25%, while adjusted EBITDA is expected to remain roughly flat.

Valuation

To assess SE’s valuation, let us look at a set of traditional price multiples. On one hand, SE appears to be trading at a significant premium compared to the consumer discretionary. On the other hand, it seems cheap compared to its historic metrics.

We have to understand that SE is a growth stock that is delivering strong growth figures across all of its segments, and normally that comes at a premium. I believe that this premium is justified, as long as the firm executes its strategy well.

We also need to note that while the firm is not paying dividends, it is committed to returning value to its shareholders through buybacks. Currently they have a $1 billion share buyback program authorised.

In the current market environment, it is also important that the company’s capital structure seems robust. They have more than $10 billion cash on hand, while the debt is roughly $3.4 billion. Having this much cash allows the firm to have financial flexibility even during times of market distress.

All in all, I believe that SE’s valuation is attractive based on the fundamentals and the growth, but of course there are also some risks that need to be considered. Let us look at them now.

Risks

There are also risks that we have to talk about.

First, SE has been issuing significant amount of shares in recent years. While this is normal for fast-growing companies, I believe that diluting shareholders is always something to be aware of and pay attention to, as eventually it eats into the returns of the investor.

Second, increased credit loss provisions. The significance of this is already visible from the reaction of the market, when the results came out and the share price plummeted, despite the strong growth figures. To be precise here, SE’s credit losses totalled $393 million in Q4, up 67% from the prior year, driven by Monee’s uncollected loans. As investors it is important to monitor how the loan portfolio develops. It is one thing that the firm managed to grow its outstanding loan principal by 60% year-over-year, but the quality of these loans is another question. Considering the current macroeconomic landscape with low consumer confidence globally, and the far-reaching consequences of the Strait of Hormuz, people may be inclined to take on more debt, but it may be much harder for them to repay it. This presents a significant risk.

Last, but not least, elevated energy prices are also likely to impact SE. In 2025, we already saw that high server and hosting expenses ate into the profit. With high oil prices and a high demand for computing, I do not believe that these headwinds are going to ease in the near term, especially considering that a deal was not reached between the U.S. and Iran yet.

Conclusions

I like SE’s business due to the robust, broad-based growth across all of its segments. The firm is delivering on its promises and scales each of the segments successfully. Shopee’s GMV and revenue are growing, Monee’s loan portfolio is expanding, and Garena’s bookings are also going up.

I believe that the valuation is currently justified, even if it is higher than the consumer discretionary sector median. Strong growth, strong profitability, robust capital structure, and the $1 billion share buyback program all justify the current price, in my view.

Risks always exist, however. The increasing number of shares - meaning the dilution of existing shareholders - needs to be kept in mind. The elevated energy prices and the low consumer confidence around the globe could also pressure SE’s business. Last, but not least, the increased credit loss provisions also need to be kept in mind.

All in all, I believe that SE’s business is attractive at the current price, despite the risks; therefore, I rate the company’s stock as buy.

data.title: The Credit Market Lens: Oil Supply Shocks Don't Age Well
data.description:
Markets may be pricing some relief for now, but the true measure of an oil shock is how long it endures.

The Middle East ceasefire sparked a relief rally last week as markets dialed back the risk of a deep, drawn-out oil supply shock. Stocks have already erased much of the post-conflict drop. Bonds haven’t gotten the memo: Yields are still elevated, keeping a bit of extra term premium on the table.

That gap is telling. Bond investors are still pricing a messier growth-inflation mix than the pre-conflict baseline. And crude isn’t exactly flashing “all clear,” either, with spot prices still near $100 a barrel as of this writing.

History is blunt on this point: It’s not the oil spike that breaks risk appetite, it’s how long it sticks around. Figures 1 and 2 illustrate this pattern by comparing cumulative changes in spot and six-month crude futures across the four major oil supply shocks of the past four decades: the 1990 Gulf War, the 2000 OPEC production cuts, the 2022 Russia–Ukraine conflict, and the current Middle East war. Just over a month into the current episode, both spot and six-month futures have tracked a path remarkably similar to the early phase of the 1990 Gulf War.

Figure 1: Spot WTI prices have followed an almost identical pattern to the 1990 Gulf War

Figure 2: The same is true for the six-month contract

The bottom line is that it was only as the 1990 conflict dragged on – energy infrastructure was taken offline and excess inventories depleted – that markets began to sharply reprice longer-dated oil futures. That inflection coincided with material widening of U.S. dollar investment-grade credit spreads (see Figure 3) and lower Treasury yields, as investors increasingly treated the shock as a negative impulse for economic growth.

Figure 3: USD IG credit spreads have not materially widened since the start of the current conflict

Whether the current episode follows a similar trajectory remains uncertain – but if it does, time is not your friend.

A potentially deeper K-shaped divide

The K-shaped economy narrative has regained momentum as higher gas prices weigh more heavily on lower-income U.S. households than on others. As of year-end 2025, data from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York show credit card and auto loan delinquencies near two-decade highs (see Figure 4), despite low unemployment and healthy aggregate metrics such as debt service and debt-to-income ratios.

Figure 4: Credit card and auto loan delinquencies are near two-decade highs

Lower-income households have largely exhausted excess savings and remain disproportionately exposed to elevated prices for essentials such as rent, insurance, and healthcare, while higher interest rates have raised debt servicing costs and tightened access to credit. The result is a bifurcated consumer: resilience at the top of the income spectrum and growing stress at the bottom.

Nowhere is this more apparent than in the auto loan market, where asset-backed securities (ABS) data indicate that the rise in delinquencies has been driven almost entirely by subprime borrowers (see Figure 5). At the start of the year, consensus economic expectations centered on stronger growth, easing inflation, and lower monetary policy rates helping to relieve pressure on subprime borrowers. The current backdrop challenges that narrative, leaving lower‑income households even more exposed.

Figure 5: The rise in auto loan delinquencies has been almost entirely driven by subprime borrowers

This bifurcation does not mean higher-income U.S. households are fully insulated from an economic slowdown. Here again, data from the Federal Reserve Board show that the combined value of household real estate equity and equity shares, a measure of household wealth tied to the housing and stock markets, has increased roughly fourfold since the end of the global financial crisis, far outpacing nominal GDP (see Figure 6).

Figure 6: U.S. household wealth linked to housing and the stock market has surged

To put this in context, as a share of nominal GDP, the value of household real estate equity and equity share has risen from about 120% at year-end 2009 to roughly 271% as of year-end 2025, the highest level on record (see Figure 7).

Figure 7: Market value of household real estate equity and equity share has more than doubled as a share of GDP

This strong reliance on asset-based wealth creates its own vulnerabilities. Abrupt tightening in financial conditions, particularly via lower stock market prices, could trigger larger wealth effects than in the past, which could ultimately lead to weaker consumption and aggregate demand.

Securitized products: Strong buffers, sharper fault lines

Despite the continued deterioration in fundamentals among lower-income households, the ABS market appears largely unfazed. At face value, spreads in benchmark ABS have shown little to no movement year-to-date. Does that signal complacency? Not necessarily.

The strong credit enhancement levels in senior ABS tranches – those highest in the capital structure – remain exceptionally robust, providing substantial buffer against underlying credit stress. That said, the primary market, where new deals are sold, is beginning to reveal a more nuanced picture. There, a clear bifurcation has emerged between prime and subprime collateral, with investors demanding greater compensation for lower-quality exposure.

The implication is straightforward: While headline spreads may appear stable, dispersion is likely to increase under the surface. In this environment, staying higher up in the capital structure and focusing on quality remain key.

Michael Puempel and Gabriel Cazaubieilh contributed to this report.
